title: Dog Sudden Inability to Walk on Back Legs: Emergency Triage Guide
meta_desc: Sudden hind limb paralysis in dogs is a high-priority emergency. Learn about IVDD, symptoms, and why you must seek immediate veterinary care.
content_html:
TL;DR: Sudden hind limb paralysis is a critical medical emergency that requires immediate veterinary intervention to prevent permanent neurological damage. If your dog cannot walk or is dragging their back legs, proceed to an emergency hospital right away.

What causes my dog to suddenly lose use of their back legs?

  • Sudden inability to walk (hind limb paresis or paralysis) occurs due to a communication disruption between the brain and the rear limbs.
  • Symptoms often include dragging the legs, "knuckling" over on the paws, or a complete collapse of the hindquarters.
  • Common underlying causes include Intervertebral Disc Disease (IVDD), spinal trauma, or a "spinal stroke" (Fibrocartilaginous Embolism).

Is sudden back leg paralysis in dogs a medical emergency?

  • Yes, this is a high-urgency medical emergency indicating potential spinal cord compression or neurological damage.
  • Time is critical; early treatment involving emergency surgery or intensive anti-inflammatory therapy significantly improves the prognosis for recovery.
  • If your dog is unable to walk, you should proceed to the nearest emergency veterinary hospital immediately.

How can a photo or video help the vet triage my dog's condition?

  • If it is safe to do so, capturing a quick video of your dog attempting to move provide critical information to the triage team.
  • Veterinarians use these visuals to look for specific neurological cues, such as paw positioning and weight-bearing ability.
  • Documentation allows the medical team to assess the urgency level and prepare diagnostics before you arrive at the clinic.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Acute, painful paraparesis and inability to flex or extend the hock can be caused by arterial thromboembolism, most commonly at the distal aortic trifurcation, resulting in ischemia of muscles and nerves in the pelvic limbs. Clinical signs include loss of flexor reflex and, in some cases, the patellar reflex, decreased sensation distal to the hock, firm and painful gastrocnemius and cranial tibial muscles, cyanotic nails, and weak or absent femoral pulses. Severe thoracolumbar spinal cord injury may cause paraplegia with increased extensor tone in the thoracic limbs (Schiff-Sherrington phenomenon), and radiographs may demonstrate vertebral fractures and luxations. Rapidly progressive flaccid tetraparesis or tetraplegia, potentially with facial and laryngeal weakness, can occur due to coonhound paralysis, initially presenting as a short-strided gait in the pelvic limbs; spinal cord reflexes are weak to absent, and muscle atrophy develops rapidly.

Chapter: Cardiology, Neurology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1245)

faqs.question: What are the most common causes of sudden back leg failure?
faqs.answer: The most frequent causes include Intervertebral Disc Disease (IVDD), Fibrocartilaginous Embolism (FCE), and sudden spinal cord trauma or injury.

faqs.question: Can a dog recover from sudden paralysis?
faqs.answer: Many dogs recover with prompt veterinary intervention, which may include surgery, medication, and physical therapy, depending on the specific diagnosis and how quickly they receive care.

faqs.question: How should I transport a paralyzed dog?
faqs.answer: Keep your dog's spine as straight and still as possible. Use a flat board, a sturdy blanket as a stretcher, or a supportive sling to minimize movement and prevent further spinal damage during transport.
